results.title: Karnataka notifies 499 acres for GBIT, India's first AI City near Bengaluru
results.description: Despite opposition from farmers and political parties, the Karnataka government has issued the final notification to acquire nearly 500 acres for the first phase of the proposed Greater Bengaluru Integrated Township, popularly known as India's First AI City. The Karnataka government has taken a major step towards implementing the proposed Greater Bengaluru Integrated Township (GBIT) near Bidadi by issuing the final notification for land acquisition in the project's first phase. The decision comes despite continued protests and objections from farmers, landowners and several political parties. The move signals the government's determination to push ahead with one of the most ambitious urban development projects championed by Deputy Chief Minister D.K. Shivakumar. Apart from the proposed Bengaluru tunnel road project, GBIT is considered one of the state's largest and most politically significant infrastructure initiatives. The latest notification covers around 499 acres spread across three villages located in Ramanagara and Harohalli taluks. Also Read: ₹26,000 crore for Bengaluru, irrigation push: DK Shivakumar places 18 key demands before PM Modi Among the villages selected in the first phase, Kempayyanapalya in Bidadi Hobli accounts for the largest share of land acquisition, with approximately 367 acres identified. Another 70 acres will be acquired from Mandalahalli village, while around 61 acres have been notified in Vaderahalli village of Harohalli Hobli. The notification is expected to be the first among several acquisition notifications required for the larger township project. The Greater Bengaluru Integrated Township, or GBIT, is a mega urban development project planned near Bidadi in Bengaluru South district. Estimated to cost more than ₹20,000 crore, the project has been described by the state government as "India's First AI City." The township is envisioned as a large-scale integrated urban hub where people can live, work and access recreation within a single planned ecosystem. The full project is proposed across approximately 7,481 acres covering nine villages in Ramanagara and Harohalli taluks. The villages likely to be impacted by the larger project include Bhyramangala, Bannigiri, Hosur, Vaderahalli, Kanchugaranahalli, Kempayyanapalya, KG Gollarapalya, Mandalahalli and Aralalusandra. The state cabinet had granted final approval to the project about a month ago, paving the way for land acquisition. Earlier, after receiving formal approval in February 2025, the Greater Bengaluru Development Authority issued a preliminary notification in March 2025 proposing acquisition of land for the project. Also Read: Feeding stray dogs? Follow the rules or face action, says Greater Bengaluru Authority According to the final notification, all objections submitted by landowners and stakeholders were examined before the government decided to proceed further. The government has authorised the Commissioner of the Greater Bengaluru Development Authority and the Special Land Acquisition Officer-II of GBDA, Ramanagara, to complete the acquisition process. The notification further states that if there are differences between Akar Bandh records and pahani records, the extent mentioned in the Akar Bandh records will be considered final. It also clarifies that ownership issues involving government survey numbers and unregularised land grants will be resolved according to a 2012 government circular. The government has proposed compensation ranging from ₹2.07 crore to ₹2.5 crore per acre for acquired land. In addition to monetary compensation, eligible landowners may also choose an alternative model under which they can receive up to 50 percent share in the developed land within the township project. The township has been designed as a technology-driven urban centre with several large-scale infrastructure and lifestyle components. Key proposals include AI and technology-based industrial zones, AI-enabled residential communities, global-standard schools, advanced medical facilities, skill development centres and large employment opportunities expected to generate lakhs of jobs. The master plan also includes 17 lakes, around 1,080 acres of parks and green spaces, entertainment and sports facilities, dedicated event and concert zones, and zero-traffic mobility corridors aimed at improving urban transportation. Also Read: Metro–suburban rail link at Benniganahalli set to supercharge East Bengaluru realty Landowners seeking compensation will have to submit several documents during the acquisition process. These include certified copies of sale deeds, partition deeds, gift deeds, will deeds or other ownership documents, family tree certificates issued by the Deputy Tahsildar, proof of land revenue payment and identity documents such as Aadhaar Card, Voter ID or PAN Card along with passport-size photographs. Applicants must also provide an indemnity bond on ₹500 bond paper, an affidavit on ₹100 bond paper, consent certificates from family members, bank account details with attested passbook copies, and an affidavit declaring details of any court cases related to the land pending before revenue authorities or courts. Even as the government proceeds with acquisition, opposition from farmers remains strong. Many landowners have expressed concerns about losing fertile agricultural land, traditional livelihoods and the rural identity of the region. However, with the final notification now issued for the first phase, the project has moved into a crucial implementation stage, bringing Karnataka's ambitious AI City vision closer to reality.
